steps.title: Identify the grammar rule
steps.content: Subject-verb agreement. The subject is Mathematician Grigori Perelman — singular. The phrase "sometimes in conjunction with mathematicians Richard S. Hamilton and Shing-Tung Yau" is a parenthetical interrupter set off by commas; it does not add to the subject. Prepositional phrases beginning with in conjunction with, along with, as well as, etc. never make a singular subject plural.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• "are": Plural present — violates subject-verb agreement; treats the parenthetical as adding to the subject, which it does not. ✗
  • "are being": Plural progressive — same number error as A, and the progressive aspect is inappropriate for a long-established attribution. ✗
  • "have been": Plural present perfect — wrong number; also implies the credit is no longer current, contradicting the passage's present context. ✗
  • "is": Singular simple present — correctly agrees with the singular subject "Perelman." ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
